2000.assessment: Debt ~55% GDP; manageable deficit. HIPC initiative in process. IMF PRGF program. Fiscal situation adequate with donor support.

2000.assessment: GDP per capita $219. Burkina Faso remains among world's poorest. Cotton and gold exports. Compaoré stable governance.

2009.assessment: $512. Global crisis barely affects Burkina Faso (not highly integrated).

2010.assessment: $589. Gold production increases significantly; Compaoré government.

2013.assessment: $716. Good economic growth. Near best years.

2014.assessment: $713. October uprising barely affects economy; transition smooth enough economically.

2015.assessment: $643. Some decline; transition year; security concerns beginning. Cotton prices down.

2016.assessment: $650. Kaboré government; growth resuming. Gold strong.

2018.assessment: $754. Good growth; gold prices high. Security crisis not yet fully impacting GDP.

2019.assessment: $796. Growth slowing as security crisis worsens. Agricultural disruption.

2020.assessment: $761. COVID-19 + security crisis causes contraction. Agricultural disruption.

2021.assessment: $858. Recovery; gold prices high. Despite crisis, Ouagadougou economy relatively intact.

2022.assessment: $869. Near peak despite two coups; gold exports continue (mines in west/south still operating).

2023.assessment: $855 estimated. Gold mining continues; agricultural sector severely disrupted by conflict.

2024.assessment: $840 estimated. Security crisis impact on agriculture and supply chains. Sanctions risk from ECOWAS.

2025.assessment: Estimated $830. Continued security crisis impact despite gold revenues.

2000.assessment: Deflation -0.3%; stable prices. CFA franc pegged to EUR providing stability.

2000.assessment: ~37% trade/GDP. Burkina Faso landlocked; cotton and gold exports; limited trade openness.

2014.assessment: ~52%. October uprising; trade not significantly affected.

2019.assessment: ~45%. Security crisis beginning to affect border trade.

2020.assessment: ~42%. COVID-19 reduces trade.

2021.assessment: ~44%. Recovery.

2022.assessment: ~40%. Security crisis + coups; some trade disruption.

2023.assessment: ~38%. Significant decline; AES isolation (ECOWAS suspended Burkina Faso) + security crisis restricts trade. France expelled.

2024.assessment: ~36% estimated. Continued trade restrictions from ECOWAS sanctions and security disruptions.

2025.assessment: Estimated ~35%; continued restrictions from regional isolation and conflict.

2000.assessment: Unemployment very high; formal sector minimal. ~80-85% in agriculture/informal. ILO formal unemployment ~3% misleading.

2004.assessment: Very high; cotton and subsistence farming dominant.

2005.assessment: High; gold mining creating some formal employment.

2008.assessment: Food price crisis; urban unemployment pressures.

2010.assessment: Very high; gold mining sector growing but formal employment limited.

2012.assessment: Very high; ILO formal unemployment <5% but ~85% in informal.

2014.assessment: Very high; October uprising reflects youth unemployment frustration.

2016.assessment: High unemployment; jihadist conflict beginning to displace agricultural workers.

2017.assessment: High; security crisis worsening agricultural employment in north.

2018.assessment: Very high; ~120,000 IDPs beginning to strain labor markets.

2019.assessment: Crisis level; 500,000+ IDPs; agricultural workers displaced from conflict zones. Crisis unemployment in affected regions.

2020.assessment: Crisis; COVID-19 + conflict displacement. 1.5M IDPs. Agricultural season severely disrupted.

2021.assessment: Crisis; 1.5M+ IDPs; security crisis preventing farming in large areas.

2022.assessment: Crisis; two coups + massive displacement. 2M+ IDPs by end 2022.

2023.assessment: Crisis; 2M+ IDPs; jihadists blocking farming in large areas. Djibo blockade creates economic collapse in north.

2024.assessment: Estimated crisis; 2.5M+ IDPs; widespread food insecurity and unemployment in conflict zones.

2025.assessment: Estimated crisis-level unemployment in affected areas; ongoing displacement.

2000.assessment: Compaoré fully integrated. Burkina Faso is a key ECOWAS member; Compaoré mediates Côte d'Ivoire conflict. US, EU, France all engaged. HIPC process with IMF/WB.

2001.assessment: Full engagement. Compaoré trusted regional mediator.

2003.assessment: Full relations; Marcoussis Accords mediation role.

2005.assessment: Full relations. Compaoré mediates multiple West African conflicts.

2006.assessment: Full engagement. France maintains strong bilateral ties (Operation Barkhane predecessor).

2009.assessment: Full relations. G5 Sahel precursor discussions.

2011.assessment: Full relations; mutinies managed without international consequences.

2014.assessment: October uprising; transitional government internationally recognized. AU and ECOWAS support transition. Full engagement.

2015.assessment: Successful elections; full international recognition. RSP coup reversed internationally applauded.

2016.assessment: Kaboré government internationally engaged. G5 Sahel (2014) active; France/Barkhane operations in Burkina Faso.

2017.assessment: Full relations. G5 Joint Force for Sahel. France, EU, US engaged.

2018.assessment: Full engagement; security partnerships with France, US, EU.

2019.assessment: Full engagement despite security crisis; international attention. G5 Sahel.

2020.assessment: Full engagement. Barkhane/France present. Security crisis brings more international attention.

2021.assessment: Full relations; some criticism of government response to security crisis.

2022.assessment: Jan 2022 Damiba coup: suspended from ECOWAS; some embassies reduce staff. Sep 2022 Traoré coup: further ECOWAS response. France/Barkhane relations strained (Burkina Faso asks Barkhane to leave). Partial isolation.

2023.assessment: Traoré expels French forces (Feb 2023); French ambassador expelled. RFI/France24 banned. AES formation with Mali and Niger. ECOWAS suspended. EU/US diplomatic tensions. Significant but not total isolation.

2024.assessment: Significant isolation from West; Russia/China as alternatives. AES framework (with Mali, Niger). ECOWAS relations strained. French gone; US relations strained.

2025.assessment: Estimated significant isolation from Western actors; Russia/Wagner/China engagement growing. AES regional framework.

2000.assessment: ~1.0% GDP FDI. Gold mining exploration beginning. Relatively low for landlocked country.

2014.assessment: ~0.7%. October uprising creates uncertainty.

2019.assessment: ~1.9%. Security crisis beginning to deter investment.

2020.assessment: ~1.7%. COVID-19 + security.

2021.assessment: ~0.8%. Security crisis deterring FDI; some mines in insecure zones.

2022.assessment: ~1.2%. Two coups; investors cautious but mines continue. Some FDI continues in western gold belt.

2023.assessment: ~0.8%. Major investor pullback; France expelled; EU skeptical. Some gold mines still operating.

2024.assessment: ~0.5% estimated. Significant investor uncertainty; ECOWAS sanctions; Russian/Chinese alignment not generating equivalent FDI.

2025.assessment: Estimated ~0.5%; low FDI environment given security crisis and geopolitical realignment.

2000.assessment: Limited French military presence; bilateral security cooperation. Pre-Barkhane era.

2011.assessment: Limited. Mutinies handled by Burkina forces.

2012.assessment: Limited French cooperation. Mali crisis increasing Sahel attention.

2013.assessment: France Operation Serval in Mali (January 2013); Burkina Faso provides basing. G5 Sahel established 2014 precursor discussions. Increased French presence.

2014.assessment: G5 Sahel established; France Barkhane succeeds Serval. Burkina Faso part of G5. Significant French presence in Sahel region.

2015.assessment: France Barkhane operating in Burkina Faso territory. US SOCOM presence. G5 Joint Force.

2016.assessment: Barkhane operations expanding into Burkina Faso. US training support. Jihadist crisis brings more foreign forces.

2017.assessment: Barkhane significant presence; US trainers; G5 Sahel Joint Force (FC-G5S) operational.

2018.assessment: Barkhane + US SOCOM + G5 FC-G5S. Major international military presence responding to jihadist threat.

2019.assessment: Barkhane significant; additional forces. Multiple bilateral partners. Kaboré invited more support.

2020.assessment: Major international military presence. Takuba Task Force (EU Special Forces) beginning operations in Mali, operating near Burkina border.

2021.assessment: Significant foreign military presence; Barkhane, US, Takuba. Growing popular resentment against France in some Burkinabe circles.

2022.assessment: Jan 2022 Damiba coup; Damiba asks France to reduce presence. Sep 2022 Traoré; anti-French sentiment strong. Traoré demands Barkhane leave. US keeps some presence. Contested mixed presence.

2023.assessment: France/Barkhane fully expelled (February 2023). Wagner/Russia AEF/Corps Africa Africa advisors/fighters arrive (January-February 2023). Estimated 100-200 Wagner. US relationship strained but some limited presence. Significant but different foreign forces.

2024.assessment: Russia/Wagner (rebranded as Africa Corps) present (~200-500 personnel). AES military coordination. France completely gone. US limited presence. Russia dominant foreign military actor.

2025.assessment: Estimated Russia/Africa Corps significant presence; AES joint military operations with Mali and Niger against jihadists.

2000.assessment: Minimal displacement. Stable country. Some returning Burkinabe refugees from Côte d'Ivoire.

2002.assessment: Some cross-border displacement from Côte d'Ivoire conflict (Burkinabe living in CI).

2003.assessment: Some displacement from CI conflict; Burkinabe workers returning from Abidjan.

2011.assessment: Some displacement from food crisis and mutinies; minimal overall.

2013.assessment: Some spillover from Mali crisis; Burkina Faso receives some Mali refugees.

2014.assessment: Minimal. October uprising resolved peacefully; no mass displacement.

2015.assessment: Initial jihadist attacks; some northern villages fleeing. ~0.5%.

2016.assessment: Jihadist attacks expanding; ~1% population (190k) displaced internally.

2017.assessment: Significant displacement; ~1.5% population (300k IDPs). Some refugees to Niger/Mali.

2019.assessment: IDPs ~560k (2.7%); rapidly escalating. Many villages in Sahel region emptied.

2020.assessment: IDPs ~1M+; ~5% population. Major displacement crisis. Food insecurity 3.3M.

2021.assessment: IDPs ~1.5M; ~7% population. One of worst displacement crises in Africa. Schools, clinics closed.

2022.assessment: IDPs ~2M+; ~10% population. Two coups add to humanitarian crisis. Djibo effectively besieged. Catastrophic.

2023.assessment: IDPs ~2.1M+; >10% population. Continued JNIM/GSIM expansion. 7M food insecure. APS 2.6M (UNHCR). Catastrophic.

2024.assessment: Estimated >11% population displaced. Ongoing crisis; 2.1M+ IDPs. Famine risk in besieged areas.

2025.assessment: Estimated catastrophic; ongoing massive displacement. No near-term resolution.

2000.assessment: No sanctions. Compaoré stable; internationally recognized.

2005.assessment: No sanctions. Burkina Faso in good standing internationally.

2010.assessment: No sanctions. Compaoré mediates regional conflicts (Côte d'Ivoire, Mali).

2014.assessment: No sanctions. Oct uprising/transition recognized internationally. Transitional government accepted.

2015.assessment: No sanctions. Sept 2015 RSP coup reversed; constitutional order restored. International support maintained.

2016.assessment: No sanctions. Kaboré government internationally recognized.

2018.assessment: No sanctions. Kaboré re-engages with international community on security (France/Barkhane).

2019.assessment: No sanctions despite worsening security.

2020.assessment: No sanctions.

2021.assessment: No sanctions. Kaboré government under pressure.

2022.assessment: Jan 2022 Damiba coup: ECOWAS suspends Burkina Faso from its institutions; targeted individual travel bans on coup leaders. Sep 2022 Traoré coup: additional ECOWAS measures. Some EU/US targeted sanctions on individuals.

2023.assessment: Targeted individual sanctions maintained (ECOWAS, EU, US on junta leaders). Burkina Faso suspended from ECOWAS and AUC voting rights. No broad economic sanctions.

2024.assessment: Targeted individual sanctions. Burkina Faso joined AES (Alliance des États du Sahel) with Mali and Niger; ECOWAS relations strained. Some donors suspended bilateral aid.

2025.assessment: Estimated continued targeted sanctions; AES alternative regional framework. Traoré junta still sanctioned individually by West.

2000.assessment: FH CL score 4. Partly free; Compaoré authoritarian but not severe. Opposition parties active; press relatively open. Demonstrations tolerated.

2003.assessment: FH CL 4. Partly free; some intimidation of journalists.

2007.assessment: FH CL 4. Partly free; parliamentary elections.

2010.assessment: FH CL 4. Partly free; Compaoré re-elected.

2011.assessment: FH CL 4. Military mutinies create some restrictions; returned to partly free.

Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2013.assessment: FH CL 4. Partly free; civil society increasingly active against term limits.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2014.assessment: FH CL 4. October uprising; transition. Partly free - improved slightly with Compaoré removal.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2015.assessment: FH CL 3 (improving). Democratic transition; media opening; civil society vibrant. RSP coup reversed strengthens democratic norms.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2016.assessment: FH CL 3. Kaboré government; relatively open political environment. Free media. Opposition active.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2017.assessment: FH CL 3. Good civil liberties for Sahel region; media relatively free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2018.assessment: FH CL 3. Partly free; some security-related restrictions in north.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2019.assessment: FH CL 4. Security crisis creating more restrictions; emergency laws. Some deterioration.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2020.assessment: FH CL 4. COVID-19 + security crisis; continued restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2021.assessment: FH CL 4. Security crisis severe; some civil liberties affected. Media still partially free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2022.assessment: FH CL 5. Two coups; media shutdowns; opposition activists detained. Independent media suspended. Significant restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2023.assessment: FH CL 6. Traoré government: foreign journalists expelled; RFI/France24 banned; civil society NGOs suspended; journalists arrested. Severe restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2024.assessment: FH CL 6. Severe. Traoré expels French ambassador, journalists; anti-corruption activists arrested; APS (press bureau) suspended.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2025.assessment: Estimated FH CL 6. Continued military junta repression; severe civil liberties restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Burkina Faso Country Report

2000.assessment: Compaoré and CDP dominate; stable elite coalition. Security services, business, traditional authorities aligned with Compaoré. No serious challenge.

2009.assessment: Some tensions within CDP over term limits; functional.

2011.assessment: Military mutinies April 2011; army factionalism. Compaoré's Presidential Guard (RSP) vs regular army tensions. Active power struggles briefly.

2012.assessment: Restored stability; some CDP internal tensions over 2015 elections.

2013.assessment: Tensions over constitutional term limits; some CDP splits with MPP formation.

2014.assessment: October 2014: mass uprising + army divides. Compaoré flees. RSP initially controls then hands to civilian (Michel Kafando). Complete elite fragmentation during transition.

2015.assessment: RSP coup Sept 2015 reversed by regular army. Active power struggle between RSP (Compaoré loyalists) vs new MPP-aligned political class. RSP disbanded.

2016.assessment: Kaboré MPP government; relatively unified. CDP in opposition. Security crisis creating pressure.

2017.assessment: Kaboré coalition functional; minor tensions. Security crisis beginning to fracture consensus.

2018.assessment: Some intracoalition tensions over security failures.

2019.assessment: Security crisis worsening; opposition blaming Kaboré. Significant political competition managed.

2020.assessment: Kaboré re-elected amid security crisis; opposition challenges result. Security forces increasingly autonomous.

2021.assessment: Military increasingly frustrated with civilian government. Open factional conflict between civilian politicians and military over security strategy. Coup preparation.

2022.assessment: Jan 2022: Damiba coup. Sep 2022: Traoré ousts Damiba in second coup. Active military power struggle. MPSR divided.

2023.assessment: Traoré government consolidating but internal military tensions. Alleged coup plots. Alliance with Russia (Wagner) creates new factional dynamics.

2024.assessment: Traoré consolidating power; managed tensions. Some former coup plotters detained. Wagner/Russian advisory presence creates new power center.

2025.assessment: Estimated significant competition managed; Traoré maintaining control amid security crisis and internal military tensions.

2000.assessment: Compaoré government; institutions nominally functioning but authoritarian control. Parliament exists but rubber-stamps; judiciary not independent. 1991 constitution but Compaoré dominates.

2001.assessment: Functional but flawed; Compaoré controls institutions. CDP party dominant.

2002.assessment: Stable semi-authoritarian institutions. Some civil society space.

2004.assessment: CDP dominant; institutions functional but constrained by presidential control.

2005.assessment: Compaoré re-elected 2005; institutions nominally functioning. Independent commissions exist.

2007.assessment: Parliamentary elections 2007; CDP wins. Institutions functional.

2008.assessment: Institutions functional; civil society increasingly active.

2009.assessment: Functional but flawed; constitutional term limits controversy (Compaoré changed terms twice before).

2010.assessment: Compaoré re-elected 2010; CDP dominant. Constitutional court operational.

2011.assessment: Military mutinies April 2011; brief institutional crisis. Army, police, judicial council all disrupted. Compaoré survives but authority weakened.

2013.assessment: Senate re-established. Constitutional commission on term limits.

2014.assessment: October 2014 uprising; Compaoré flees. Constitutional Court declares presidential vacancy. CNTS (military transitional) installs civilian Lt-Gen. Isaac Zida then Michel Kafando. Institutions disrupted.

2015.assessment: September 2015 RSP coup reversed after 7 days; CNTS restored, then transitions to Kaboré. Elections November 2015. Institutions recovering.

2016.assessment: Kaboré government (MPP); institutions rebuilding. New parliament active. Constitutional Court functional.

2017.assessment: Institutions functioning but security crisis beginning to stress capacity. Burkina Faso ranked among Africa's more democratic states.

2018.assessment: Institutions functional but under increasing security pressure. Emergency areas declared. Some executive overreach.

2019.assessment: Security crisis worsens; institutions in affected regions collapsed. National institutions still functioning in capital but severely stressed. State of emergency in Sahel.

2020.assessment: Kaboré re-elected (controversial); security crisis continues. Institutions in northern half of country barely functional. State of emergency expanded.

2021.assessment: Institutions severely stressed; major areas with no government presence. Security forces overwhelmed. CODER opposition boycott.

2022.assessment: Jan 2022 coup (Damiba/MPSR); constitution suspended; National Assembly dissolved. Sep 2022 coup (Traoré); further institutional disruption. Civilian institutions severely degraded.

2023.assessment: Traoré governing by decree; parliament still suspended; independent judiciary undermined. Media shutdowns. Jihadist crisis means institutions absent from 50%+ of territory.

2024.assessment: Severely degraded; military junta governance. Independent press suppressed; civil society restricted. Some consultative bodies established by Traoré but no democratic institutions.

2025.assessment: Estimated severely degraded; Traoré government with no electoral mandate, ongoing military rule.

2000.assessment: Very low political violence. Compaoré government stable. Occasional political repression.

2003.assessment: Minimal; some cross-border tensions from Côte d'Ivoire conflict.

2005.assessment: Minimal; protests over elections but limited violence.

2008.assessment: Food riots in February 2008; ~10 killed. Security forces respond. Otherwise minimal.

2011.assessment: Military mutinies April 2011; Presidential Guard fires on protesters; ~15 killed total. Brief but serious.

2014.assessment: October uprising; Presidential Guard fires on crowd; ~15-20 killed. Compaoré flees.

2015.assessment: RSP coup September 2015; ~10 killed. First Ansarul Islam jihadist attacks in Soum province; initial deaths ~50. ACLED total ~100.

2016.assessment: Jihadist attacks expanding; ~200 deaths. Ansarul Islam founded by Ibrahim Malam Dicko.

2017.assessment: Significant expansion of jihadist violence (~500 deaths). JNIM attacks Burkina Faso from Mali. Security forces overwhelmed in north.

2018.assessment: Major escalation; ~1,200 deaths. JNIM, Ansarul Islam, GSIM attacks throughout Sahel, north, and east.

2019.assessment: Crisis escalation: ~2,500 deaths. Hundreds of villages attacked; VDP (Volunteers for Defense of Homeland) formed. 500,000+ IDPs.

2020.assessment: ~3,500 deaths. Kaboré re-elected but security crisis worsens. Massacres at Solhan (160+ killed June 2021 - year boundary issue).

2021.assessment: Worst violence yet; ~5,000 deaths including Solhan massacre (June 2021, 160+ killed). 1.5M IDPs. State losing control rapidly.

2022.assessment: Two coups + continued jihadist attacks; ~6,000 deaths. Significant massacre at Seytenga (June 2022, 80+ killed). Traoré coup Sept 2022.

2023.assessment: Continued high violence; ~8,000 deaths. Government and VDP massacres documented. Djibo supply convoy ambushed. Traoré orders mass mobilization.

2024.assessment: Very high but slight decline with Wagner/AES operations. ~7,000 deaths. Continued humanitarian crisis.

2025.assessment: Estimated ~6,000 deaths; continued high but slight improvement as AES military operations intensify.

2000.assessment: Compaoré government controls full territory. Burkina Faso stable; occasional rural banditry but no insurgency. Full government control.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2003.assessment: Full control. Some cross-border tensions from Côte d'Ivoire conflict.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2011.assessment: Brief military mutinies (April 2011); protests in Ouagadougou. Security forces briefly unstable but government control maintained.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports
International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2014.assessment: October 2014 uprising ousts Compaoré after 27 years. Presidential Guard fires on crowd; ~15 killed. CNTS transitional government. Control maintained during transition.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2015.assessment: September 2015 coup attempt by Presidential Guard (RSP) quickly reversed. First jihadist attacks in north (Soum province). Control mostly maintained but insurgency emerging.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2016.assessment: Kaboré government (MPP, from Jan 2016). Ansarul Islam forms (2016). North increasingly insecure. Government losing control of some Sahel region villages.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2017.assessment: Jihadist attacks expanding (Ansarul Islam, GSIM). Sahel and Est regions increasingly contested. ~10% territory insecure.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2018.assessment: JNIM (Group for Support of Islam and Muslims) expanding operations. ~15% territory contested. Army struggling in north and east.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2019.assessment: Major jihadist expansion. ~25% territory effectively outside government control. Entire provinces (Soum, Oudalan, Tapoa) largely ungovernable. 500,000+ IDPs.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2020.assessment: Government controls approximately half of territory effectively. Major areas (40%+ ) insecure. Kaboré re-elected but crisis deepening. COVID-19 + insurgency.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2021.assessment: Territorial control crisis. Government loses access to Djibo (Soum province capital) intermittently. Large portions contested; ~40% outside effective control.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2022.assessment: Jan 2022: Damiba coup. Sep 2022: Traoré coup. Jihadists now control 40-50% of territory. Djibo under siege. Hundreds of thousands without government services.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2023.assessment: Traoré government loses majority of territory. JNIM/GSIM/Ansarul Islam control est. 50-60% of territory. Djibo supply convoy attacked. Humanitarian crisis.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2024.assessment: Continued territorial losses. JNIM controls vast swathes of north, east, Sahel. Government in Ouagadougou and main cities. Wagner/AES (Alliance des États du Sahel) trying to push back.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2025.assessment: Estimated continued crisis. Traoré government struggling to maintain control. Alliance des États du Sahel (with Mali, Niger) but limited military success.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Burkina Faso Reports

2000.assessment: No EITI; gold revenues opaque. Mining concessions granted without public disclosure.

2003.assessment: No EITI; HIPC process improving some transparency.

2004.assessment: No EITI; gold revenues not publicly disclosed.

2008.assessment: Major gold exports; no EITI yet. Ministry of Mines tracks but no public disclosure.

2009.assessment: Burkina Faso becomes EITI candidate (2009). Gold, zinc, manganese revenues being reconciled.

2011.assessment: EITI candidate; multiple EITI reports covering gold mines.

2013.assessment: Burkina Faso EITI compliant (2013). Gold revenues meaningfully disclosed; 14+ operating mines reporting.

2014.assessment: EITI compliant; meaningful gold/zinc/manganese disclosures. October uprising no impact on EITI.

2015.assessment: EITI compliant; active reporting. Kaboré government supports transparency.

2017.assessment: EITI compliant; gold revenues growing and being disclosed.

2018.assessment: EITI compliant; beneficial ownership work beginning.

2019.assessment: EITI compliant; EITI 2019 standard. Gold revenues ~$2B+ annually disclosed.

2020.assessment: EITI compliant; COVID-19 but gold mines continue.

2021.assessment: EITI validation 2021; corrective actions required (beneficial ownership, contract transparency).

2022.assessment: Two coups; EITI Board considers status. Burkina Faso placed on EITI watchlist/suspended status. Junta commitment to transparency unclear.

2023.assessment: EITI status under review; junta governance raising concerns about transparency standards. Some EITI reports still published but with reduced credibility.

2024.assessment: Estimated suspended/under review; military junta has not fulfilled EITI governance requirements. Gold mines still operating (western belt) but revenues less transparent.

2025.assessment: Estimated suspended or watchlist; EITI compliance cannot be certified under military junta governance.

2000.assessment: Compaoré era; legal framework exists but enforcement arbitrary. Compaoré controls judiciary.

2000.assessment: Compaoré era; press problematic. Some independent newspapers. State radio dominant. Journalists occasionally detained.

2003.assessment: Relatively open for Sahel; some independent press.

2006.assessment: Improving; relatively good for West Africa.

2011.assessment: Mutinies create some press restrictions; setback.

2013.assessment: Improving; Burkina Faso among better performers in Francophone Africa.

2014.assessment: October uprising; media plays major role. Press freedom improves post-Compaoré.

2015.assessment: RSP coup reversed; democratic transition; press very active. Best period for Burkina press.

2016.assessment: Kaboré government; press relatively free; Burkina Faso in "satisfactory" range for RSF (~65).

2017.assessment: RSF improving rank; good press environment for Sahel region.

2018.assessment: Security crisis creating some tensions; some journalists attacked.

2019.assessment: Security crisis; jihadists targeting journalists. Some restrictions.

2020.assessment: COVID-19 + security; some press restrictions.

2021.assessment: Security crisis making some reporting dangerous.

2022.assessment: Two coups devastate press freedom. Military suspends critical media. RSF documents major deterioration. Score drops sharply.

2023.assessment: Traoré government: France24/RFI banned; foreign journalists expelled; local journalists arrested; internet shutdowns. Very serious press freedom situation.

2024.assessment: Continued severe press restrictions. Multiple journalists arrested; some disappeared. RSF ranks Burkina Faso among worst in West Africa.

2025.assessment: Estimated very serious; military junta continuing press repression.

2000.assessment: WB SCI not yet available; estimated moderate. INSD (Institut National de la Statistique et de la Démographie) functional. 1996 Population Census used.
